notes: One login now fully enables cross-site blog sharing. Small but important fix to 1.14.75: the server rule that lets OTHER sites read this site's posts is installed by the login self-heal, which runs once per browser tab — so the very login that installed the engine update would skip it, and cross-site reading only switched on at the NEXT fresh sign-in. Updates now clear that gate, so the same login that brings in the new engine also enables the posts feed right after the update reload. If a group site's blog looked empty after setting a source, sign in to the SOURCE site once on this version and it will light up. Previous (1.14.75) — One site's blog can now feed every site in the group. The post archive block has a new optional source: point it at another site (e.g. your flagship 44idigital.com) and it shows THAT site's published posts — newest first, updating automatically whenever the source publishes, with every card linking to the full article on the source site. Setup on each receiving site: Posts tab → “Add post list to a page” → enter the source address in the new “Pull posts from a different site” field → pick the page. One requirement on the SOURCE site: it must be on this engine version and have been signed into once — signing in switches on cross-site reading of its public posts feed (the same login self-heal that installs clean URLs; it opens exactly one file, the public posts list, to other sites — nothing else). Also works for non-CMS sites via a small paste-in script your agency can provide. Previous (1.14.74) — The live post archive now looks like a real blog section — and you can swap an old posts section for it in one click. (1) Post List cards follow the classic blog-archive design: featured image on top, small uppercase date, bold title, a short excerpt (long ones neatly clamp to four lines so cards stay even), and a “Read more →” link that automatically picks up your site's brand color when the page defines one. Converted posts (pages you turned into posts) appear right alongside written ones — they're all just posts. (2) New in the page editor: select your old hand-made posts section and click “Replace with post archive” — the section is swapped for the live list in place (Undo brings it back until you leave the page), then Save publishes it. “Insert post list” is still there for placing the archive anywhere fresh. Also fixed: inserting a post list into a page that already had a form skipped the list's styling in the editor preview. Previous (1.14.73) — Your blog page can now show ALL your posts automatically — newest first, always current. Until now, a site's own Blog page was just a normal page: any post links on it were hand-made, so new posts written in the Posts tab never appeared there (the built-in posts.html archive knew about them, but the site's styled Blog page did not). New in this release: a live Post List block you can put on any page. It reads your posts at view time and always shows every published post in chronological order (newest first, using each post's date — editable in the post editor), with the featured image, date, title, and excerpt, linking into the full article. Two ways to use it: (1) Posts tab → “Add post list to a page” → pick your Blog page — done in one click, the page keeps its own design and address; (2) in the page editor, click an element and use “Insert post list” to place it exactly where you want. The list updates by itself every time you publish, unpublish, or re-date a post — no page editing needed. If your Blog page still has old hand-made post links, open it in the editor and delete them; the live list replaces them. Previous (1.14.72) — Favicons now actually come through — everywhere, every time. The favicon uploader (Settings → Site Identity) was quietly producing broken results in four ways, all fixed: (1) it baked the admin's exact web address into every page, so the icon broke whenever a site was reached via www vs non-www, http vs https, or when a site was cloned — the icon now lives at the site root as /favicon and pages reference it with a root-relative address that works everywhere; (2) iPhones ignored SVG and ICO home-screen icons and showed a blank tile — tab (32px), Android/Google (192px), and iPhone (180px, white-backed) PNG sizes are now generated automatically from whatever you upload; (3) browsers cache favicons for weeks, so replacing one looked like the upload failed — each upload now publishes under a fresh versioned address that browsers must re-fetch; (4) pages created AFTER the favicon was set never received it — every page save now stamps the current favicon automatically, and there's an explicit “Apply to all pages” button for re-stamping the whole site. Old absolute-address favicons are converted to the new form the next time anything applies. Previous (1.14.71) — Image picker tiles are now exactly 250×250 with the full image contained inside — and the REAL cause of the squashed picker is fixed. A screenshot from the affected machine (running v1.14.70, with no diagnostic bar raised) finally identified the culprit: the tiles themselves were always full-size — the browser was collapsing the GRID'S ROWS, so every row of tiles painted stacked on top of the previous one and only a thin strip of each tile showed. All previous hardening watched the tiles; the rows were the blind spot. The CMS now guards the grid container too: if rows are forced small or tiles start overlapping, the row layout is re-asserted instantly, and tile measurement now uses the PAINTED size, so even squashes done with transforms or zoom are caught and reversed. The picker's tiles are 250×250 per request (the whole image visible inside each tile), and the red diagnostic bar — should anything still misbehave — now reports the grid's row settings and row spacing so the culprit is named in the screenshot. Previous (1.14.70) — The media pickers can no longer be squashed by the browser — and they now diagnose themselves if anything interferes. On one live machine the image tiles kept collapsing into thin strips even after three rounds of CSS hardening, which points at a browser extension rewriting page styles with rules the page itself cannot outrank. Every media grid (the page editor's image picker, the featured-image picker, the block editor's Choose Image, and the main Media Library) now reserves each tile's height STRUCTURALLY — a built-in invisible spacer image holds the box open, and no stylesheet, extension or otherwise, can flatten it — so tiles stay tall and clickable in any environment. The CMS also keeps watching the grids and instantly re-asserts the layout if something tampers with it after render; if tiles still end up squashed, a red diagnostic bar appears right in the picker with the exact measurements (screenshot that bar when reporting). Each picker now shows the engine version it's running (e.g. “engine v1.14.70” in the picker footer), so a stale cached tab is instantly recognizable. And after an engine update the CMS reloads onto a version-stamped address, so the browser can never serve the old cached admin page and make a successful update look like it didn't apply. Previous (1.14.69) — Image tiles are now tall, fixed-size, and impossible to squash. On some machines the picker's thumbnails still collapsed into thin strips — something in that browser environment was overriding the tiles' size rules. The tiles now use an explicit fixed height (picker 190px, library 150px), and after every render the CMS MEASURES each tile and, if anything in the browser squashed it, forcefully re-asserts the size with the strongest override CSS allows — so the tiles win even against interfering browser extensions or injected styles. (If a tile ever still has to be rescued this way, a diagnostic line is written to the browser console so the culprit can be identified.) The full image remains visible inside each tile. Previous (1.14.68) — Image tiles always show the full picture, and the page list is alphabetical. (1) The image picker's thumbnails could still render as thin strips on some setups — the tile geometry now uses a technique every browser honors, so every thumbnail sits in a proper square tile on a visible backdrop, with the WHOLE image shown (wide banner images appear as a full-width strip inside their tile instead of a floating sliver). The main Media Library grid got the same hardening. A thumbnail that can't load still shows a selectable “preview unavailable” tile. (2) The page editor's page list is now alphabetical — Home stays pinned to the top, and everything else sorts A→Z (numbers sort naturally, case doesn't matter), so long page lists are scannable instead of import-order soup. Previous (1.14.67) — The Media Library and image pickers now handle huge libraries — and a filename quirk that could break them is fixed. Two problems, one release. (1) FIXED — the image picker could render as a wall of thin unclickable slivers: a single filename containing an apostrophe (like john's-photo.jpg) corrupted the picker's markup from that tile onward, breaking every image after it. Selection no longer embeds the file's address in the markup at all, so any filename is safe. (2) Large libraries no longer overwhelm the browser: the image pickers and the Media Library grid now load 80–120 tiles at a time with a “Show more” button (the search box still searches everything), tiles hold their size while thumbnails load instead of collapsing, and a thumbnail that can't load shows “preview unavailable” — still selectable — instead of silently vanishing. Double-click an image in the editor's picker to select and use it in one go. Previous (1.14.66) — GitHub backups now work for everyone, from every browser — and failures are visible. Until now, pushing page saves and revision snapshots to GitHub happened in the BROWSER using the Architect-only access token: anyone signed in below Architect level (admins, editors) silently skipped the GitHub copy, which is why files could exist on the server but never appear in the repo. Now the SERVER does the pushing, using the GitHub token already stored encrypted in the site's database — so every page save, every revision snapshot (data/revisions.json + data/rev_….html), and the page list mirror to GitHub no matter who saves or from which browser. The token itself never leaves the server for non-Architect users. The browser-side push remains as a fallback for sites whose server has no token saved yet. And when a mirror genuinely fails (bad token, wrong repo, rate limit), the save toast now says so — “Saved to server ✓ — GitHub mirror failed: …” — instead of whispering into the browser console. (Architects: re-save your GitHub token once in Settings → GitHub Integration if it was only ever stored in an old browser.) Previous (1.14.65) — One-click image optimization in the Media Library. New “Optimize All Images” button at the top of the Media Library: every JPEG, PNG, and WebP on the site is scaled to a web-sensible size (longest edge 2000px), re-compressed at web quality, and stripped of camera metadata (EXIF/GPS/print DPI — the result is the web-standard 72dpi), then saved over the original file — same name, same address, so your pages keep working and just load faster. Photos whose rotation lived in camera metadata are straightened first so nothing turns sideways. An image is only replaced when the optimized version is genuinely smaller (at least 5% — already-optimized files are left untouched), animations (GIF) and vector files (SVG) are never touched, and small files are skipped as not worth it. Each image also gets its own “Optimize for web” button in the image viewer, with a before/after readout (e.g. “3.2 MB → 480 KB, 85% smaller”). Progress shows while a bulk run works through the library, and the summary reports the total space saved. Previous (1.14.64) — Convert a post back into a page. Every post in the Posts list now has a “→ Page” button: it rebuilds the post as a real page — the post's content wrapped in your site's own header, footer, and styling — saves it under the post's address (a suffix is added if a page with that name already exists), adds it to the page editor's list and the sitemap, and then offers to delete the post so it stops showing on the blog. A form in the post becomes the real working form on the page. This is the undo for a page that was converted to a post by mistake (for example a blog archive page that got swept up in a bulk conversion and then deleted on the pages side): click “→ Page” on that post and it's a normal page again. Previous (1.14.63) — Turn existing pages into blog posts. Posts has a new “Convert pages to posts” button: it lists every page on the site with pages named blog… (blog-my-story.html, /blog/…, a “Blog …” title) already ticked — and you can tick ANY page to designate it as a post. Each converted page becomes a real, editable post: its content is read into the post editor's blocks (headings, paragraphs, images with captions, lists, quotes, YouTube/Vimeo embeds, dividers — and a form on the page becomes the post's form), the title/description/featured image are pulled from the page's own metadata, and anything the blocks can't represent is preserved exactly in a new HTML block (also available in the post editor's Add-block menu now). Two options at convert time: publish immediately (on by default) and delete the original pages afterwards (off by default — leaving them keeps their old addresses working while you check the posts over). Duplicate addresses are handled automatically. After converting, the blog page (posts.html) refreshes itself. Previous (1.14.62) — Feed your CRM automations the exact fields they need — match a website form to a CRM form with its share link. Until now a website submission reached your CRM as a contact with just name/email/phone (everything else only in a note), so an automation that keys on fields like “Partner Type” or “Market / City” never got them. Now, in the form builder (when Lead Generation is on) there's a “Match a CRM form” card: paste the CRM form's share link (the …/widget/form/… link), click Fetch & match fields, and the CMS reads that form's real definition — its standard fields AND its custom fields, including dropdown options and hidden fields. Each CRM field is auto-paired with your form's fields by name (Company ↔ Business name, etc.); adjust any pairing from a dropdown, or one-click “Add as a new field” to pull a missing CRM field (like a Partner Type dropdown, options included) into your form. Click Apply matching, then Save. From then on every submission files each answer into the CRM's REAL fields — standard ones (name, email, phone, company) and custom fields by their exact IDs — and hidden CRM fields (like a “Source Page” tracking value) are sent automatically. Your automations fire with the data they key on; the full submission still lands in Entries, the email notification, and the contact note as before. ALSO NEW — phone numbers can format themselves as (555) 123-4567: a “Format phone numbers” option in the form builder's settings (on by default for new forms; turn it on for an existing form and Save) makes phone fields format live as the visitor types. Numbers typed with a leading + (international) are left alone. Previous (1.14.61) — Converted forms now look like YOUR site, and Form placements works on real-world pages. (1) NEW — the CMS grabs the original form's styling. When you convert a form (single, “Convert all”, or via Form placements), the CMS renders the actual page in the background, reads how the original form really looked — input borders and rounding, colors, fonts, the button's color/shape/size, placeholder color, the box around the form — and bakes it into the managed form's Custom CSS, so the swapped-in form matches the site design instead of showing browser-default fields. (A form built with placeholders instead of labels also stays that way.) The captured CSS lands in the form's Custom CSS box, so you can tweak it. (2) FIXED — Form placements sometimes updated nothing: if a page contained a commented-out old form (or a form inside a script template), the tool miscounted which form was which and the swap missed. Both the scan and the swap now count forms the same way, skipping comments/scripts, so Apply lands on the right form every time. (3) FIXED — a form inserted in the page editor showed as tiny unstyled fields until the page was saved and reopened; the form's stylesheet now rides along, so it looks right immediately. Previous (1.14.60) — Forms now actually go onto pages — three ways. (1) FIXED: pasting a form's shortcode ([form id=…]) into a page did nothing when the page published — pages never rendered shortcodes (only blog posts did). Now, when you Save a page, any form shortcode becomes the real working form, and the form's styling + submit machinery is added to the page automatically. (2) NEW — Insert a form from the page editor: click where you want it (a section or column works best), then use the “Insert a form” button in the right-hand panel and pick from your forms list — the form drops into the page and goes live when you Save. (3) NEW — Form placements (Forms → Form placements): one table of EVERY form across ALL your pages — managed and hand-coded — each with a dropdown to point that spot at the right managed form, plus “Set all to …” for across-the-board changes; click Apply and the pages are rewritten. Perfect for the just-converted-13-forms case: point every page at the 2 real forms, then use the built-in “Delete unused forms” button to clean up the leftover duplicates (it protects forms used by blog posts, and past entries stay viewable under Entries). Previous (1.14.59) — The email delivery test is now in Settings too. Settings has a new “Contact-Form Email” card (admins only): enter an address (or leave it blank to use your own account email) and click Send test email — the CMS sends a real message down the exact same path your website forms use and shows precisely what happened, including the exact failure stage when delivery is broken (a rejected password, a blocked port, a sender the relay won't accept). It's the same trusted diagnostic that lives in Plugins → Email delivery — now findable from Settings as well, since that's where people look first. Previous (1.14.58) — Block-rendered pages and their forms are fixed, plus faster bulk form setup. (1) Two coding errors in the page-block renderer could stop a block-built page — and any form on it — from displaying at all; both are fixed, so those pages render again. (2) The form on a block-built page now actually sends: it posts to your site's own contact endpoint with no extra setup, shows a clear inline error if something goes wrong (instead of a browser pop-up), and re-enables the button afterward. (3) New bulk tools when you convert existing page forms into managed forms (Forms → “Convert a page form”): “Convert all” turns every form the scan finds into a managed form in one click, and “Use on all pages” puts one chosen form on every page — so a site with the same prototype form repeated across many pages is set up in seconds, and every submission lands in Forms → Entries. (To diagnose an “Email could not be sent” message, use the Send test email tool in Plugins → Email delivery — it shows the exact SMTP/Mailgun reason. Submissions are always saved under Forms → Entries even if the notification email fails.) Previous (1.14.57) — Forms submit again when reCAPTCHA is on — a broken setup no longer blocks real people. Two fixes. (1) Clicking Submit was doing nothing on some sites: the form waited on reCAPTCHA before it even showed “Sending…”, and if the site key was wrong (for example a v2 key used on a v3 setup) the reCAPTCHA call could hang forever, so the button appeared dead. Now the button responds instantly and the reCAPTCHA step gives up after a few seconds instead of freezing — the form always submits. (2) reCAPTCHA no longer treats everyone as a bot when it's misconfigured. Before, if the security token was missing or the keys were wrong, every submission was rejected — so a setup problem silently lost real leads. Now a submission is only ever blocked when Google actively scores it as a bot; if the check can't run (no token, wrong keys, or Google is unreachable) the submission is let through so you don't lose the lead — and the reason is recorded. Use the “Check last submission” button in Plugins → reCAPTCHA to see the current state: green = passed, red = blocked as a bot, and a new amber state = “went through, but reCAPTCHA isn't protecting this form yet” with the exact thing to fix (almost always: register reCAPTCHA v3 keys at google.com/recaptcha/admin and paste both the site and secret keys, then Save). Once the keys are correct, real bot-scoring turns back on automatically. Previous (1.14.56) — reCAPTCHA now tells you exactly why a form was blocked. In Plugins → reCAPTCHA (admins only) there's a new “Check last submission” button: click it and the CMS shows, right there, the result of the most recent form submission's reCAPTCHA check — whether it passed or was blocked, its score, when it happened, and the exact reason in plain language (for example “the SITE key is a v2 key being used with v3 — both keys must come from a reCAPTCHA v3 registration”, “score below your threshold”, or “no token received — the reCAPTCHA script didn't load on the page”). It also shows an at-a-glance summary of your current setup: the version (v2/v3), whether the site key and secret key are set, and your block threshold. So when submissions still aren't coming through, you can pinpoint the cause inside the CMS instead of hunting through the server's error log. (Admins and Super-Admins only; no keys or personal data are ever shown.) Previous (1.14.55) — reCAPTCHA v3 fixed — the badge shows and real visitors get through. Two problems are fixed: the little “protected by reCAPTCHA” badge now appears in the bottom-right, and legitimate form submissions are no longer blocked as bots. The cause was that the reCAPTCHA script wasn't reliably loading on the live pages, so the browser never produced a security token — and the server correctly rejected the empty token as a bot, no matter what threshold you set. Now, saving your reCAPTCHA settings publishes the reCAPTCHA v3 script into every page for you in one step (like the favicon), so the badge loads and every form automatically gets a valid token. If a submission is ever still rejected, the exact reason from Google (for example mismatched keys, or a score below your threshold) is now written to the server error log so it can be pinpointed. IMPORTANT: reCAPTCHA v3 needs its OWN keys — if you registered v2 keys, create a reCAPTCHA v3 site + secret at google.com/recaptcha/admin and paste those in Plugins → reCAPTCHA, then Save. Previous (1.14.54) — Targeted Landing Pages are now managed in their own module, not the main Pages list. City pages you generate no longer clutter the page editor's page list (or the dashboard) — they live in a new “Generated Pages” section inside the Targeted Landing Pages module, each with Edit, View, and Delete. They're still real, indexed pages in your sitemap; they're just kept out of the everyday list so it stays manageable. (If you open one to edit, it appears in the editor's list while you're on it, then tucks away again.) The list of pages you can localize also no longer offers already-generated city pages as sources. Previous (1.14.53) — Lead Generation chat now shows images. Photos and other media that come through in a conversation used to appear as a raw link (with a stray bracket stuck on the end) — they now render as actual inline images you can click to open full size. Other links in a message are kept as clickable links, and in the conversations list a media-only message shows as “🖼 Image” instead of a long URL. Previous (1.14.52) — Lead Generation: open a conversation and chat live. In the Lead Generation dashboard, click any row under Recent Conversations to open the full message thread in a chat window — inbound messages on the left, your replies on the right — and type a reply to send it straight back to that person on the same channel they used (live chat, SMS, etc.). The thread refreshes on its own every few seconds, so you can carry on a real back-and-forth with someone who's chatting with your site — including taking over from the chat bot. Everything runs through the server, so the connection token never touches the browser. (Note: sending depends on the channel the visitor is on; if a particular channel can't be replied to, the window shows exactly why.) Previous (1.14.51) — Login polish and white-label cleanup. (1) On the sign-in screen, the password box is now the same full width as the username box (it was shorter before), and the three empty decorative bars on the right-hand panel have been removed for a cleaner look. (2) White-label: the name of the underlying lead platform (“GoHighLevel”/“GHL”) no longer appears anywhere in the interface — the Lead Generation setup card and dashboard now use neutral wording (“your CRM”, “the lead connection”) so nothing off-brand shows to your team or clients. Functionality is unchanged. Previous (1.14.50) — The CMS interface now uses the new brand red. The accent color throughout the admin — buttons, links, the active menu item, focus rings, badges, and the visual editor's selection handles — has been retuned from the old ember-orange to the Fourge logo red, in both light and dark mode. It's driven by the theme's color tokens, so everything shifts together and stays consistent. (This is the CMS's own interface only — your clients' brand colors, their form and accessibility-widget colors, and their site content are untouched.) Previous (1.14.49) — Logo now matches the brand file exactly — including the wordmark. The 'Fourge' wordmark in the logo (on the sign-in screen, the top bar, and the loading screen) is now drawn from the logo file's own lettering — embedded as vector outlines — instead of being set in the CMS's interface font, so it matches the brand artwork exactly instead of approximating it. It still recolors automatically for dark mode. (This refines the new logo introduced in 1.14.48; the anvil mark and the browser-tab icon are unchanged.) Previous (1.14.48) — New Fourge logo. The Fourge brand mark is now the new anvil logo — it shows on the sign-in screen, the top bar, the loading screen, the page-transition animation, and the browser-tab icon (favicon), in both light and dark mode. (This is the CMS's own branding; it does not touch your client sites' logos.) Previous (1.14.47) — Polish — more breathing room in the SEO panel. On a page's SEO settings, the two checkboxes ('Hide this page from search results' and 'Don't follow links on this page') no longer sit flush against the 'Social sharing preview' section below them — there's now clear spacing between them. Previous (1.14.46) — New — a 'Send test email' button so email problems explain themselves. In Plugins → Email delivery (admins only), type an address and click Send test email: the CMS sends a real message down the exact same path your website forms use — your SMTP relay, or the Mailgun API — and shows precisely what happened. You see the method and mail server it used, the From address, where the mail settings came from (a site's old send.php or its config.secret.php), and, when it fails, the exact stage it failed at: a rejected password, a blocked port, a sender the relay won't accept, or a TLS problem — instead of a vague 'could not be sent'. So when a form saves an entry but the notification email never arrives, you can see why in seconds. (Admins and Super-Admins only, and no passwords are ever shown.) Previous (1.14.45) — Fix — the GoHighLevel 'Test connection' button now works (was erroring with 'Undefined constant GHL_API_BASE'). The two GoHighLevel API constants were defined lower in api.php than the point where incoming requests are handled, so they didn't exist yet when the Test (or a live lead push) ran; they're now defined up top, before any request is processed. Previous (1.14.44) — Fix — saving settings (like the GoHighLevel token) no longer fails on servers without the libsodium PHP extension. Some hosts don't have PHP's 'sodium' extension enabled, so saving an encrypted setting failed with 'Call to undefined function sodium_crypto_generichash()'. The CMS now encrypts stored secrets with libsodium when it's available and automatically falls back to OpenSSL (AES-256-GCM) when it isn't — so encrypted settings save and load on any modern host. Existing secrets saved on a libsodium host keep working; the two formats are tagged so each reads back correctly even across an engine update. Previous (1.14.43) — Lead Generation dashboard — a new left-menu module that pulls your GoHighLevel leads and conversations into the CMS. Once GoHighLevel is switched on (Plugins → Lead Generation, Super-Admin only), a new 'Lead Generation' tab appears in the left menu for your whole team: it shows your total lead count, recent leads (name, email, phone, source, date) and recent chat conversations pulled live from GoHighLevel, each with a one-click Download CSV. The tab only shows when the integration is on, so it doubles as the on/off switch for this paid add-on. The connection/keys still live in Plugins (Super-Admin only); the dashboard is view-only for everyone else and the API token never reaches the browser. (Note: the module uses a placeholder funnel icon for now — the exact brand mark drops in once provided.) Previous (1.14.42) — GoHighLevel integration — phase 1: website form submissions become GHL leads automatically. Turn it on in Plugins → GoHighLevel (Super-Admin only): paste a Private Integration Token from your GHL sub-account (Settings → Private Integrations, with the Contacts scope) plus your Location ID, and flip it on. From then on, every submission on that site is pushed into GoHighLevel as a contact — first/last name, email, and phone are detected automatically, the lead is tagged 'Website Lead' + the form name, and the full submission is attached as a note. No need to rebuild your forms in GHL. It de-duplicates by email/phone (a repeat submitter updates rather than duplicates), and it's best-effort — a GHL hiccup never blocks the form or its email notification. Security: the token is stored encrypted on the server and never reaches the browser or the site's repo; only Super-Admins can see or change the connection. There's a 'Test connection' button, and an on/off switch so you can enable it per client (it's a paid add-on). Coming next (phase 2): a dashboard that pulls your GHL leads and chat interactions back into the CMS, with CSV export. Previous (1.14.41) — reCAPTCHA v3 support (invisible, score-based, with the badge) and an adjustable block threshold. In Plugins → reCAPTCHA you can now choose the version: v2 — the “I’m not a robot” checkbox (still the default, so existing sites are untouched) — or v3, which runs invisibly, shows Google’s small “protected by reCAPTCHA” badge in the corner, and scores every submission in the background. With v3 you get a Block Threshold slider (0–1, default 0.5): submissions scoring below it are treated as bots and blocked — raise it toward 0.8–0.9 for stricter filtering, lower it to be more lenient. The score is enforced on the server, so it can’t be bypassed. Two things to know when switching to v3: it needs its OWN keys (choose reCAPTCHA v3 when you register the site at google.com/recaptcha/admin — v2 keys won’t work for v3), and the visible checkbox is replaced by the corner badge. Previous (1.14.40) — Revisions: View and Restore actually work now. If you clicked View or Restore on a saved version and nothing happened, this is the fix. Both used to depend on a browser pop-up (View) and the browser's built-in confirmation dialog (Restore) — and some browsers, especially in-app/embedded ones and strict pop-up blockers, silently block both, so the buttons looked dead even though the code was running. Now View opens the saved version in a preview window right inside the CMS (with an 'Open in new tab' option), and Restore asks you to confirm with an in-CMS dialog — neither relies on browser pop-ups or dialogs anymore. And if a saved snapshot is ever missing, you get a clear message in the preview instead of silence. Previous (1.14.39) — Top-bar cleanup, forms CSS is now what-you-see-is-what-you-get, page versions are backed up in GitHub, and reCAPTCHA is a proper plugin. (1) Top bar — the search box (which didn't do anything) is gone, and the site name at the top is now a simple link: click it to open your live site in a new tab, or — while you're editing a page — that exact page (same target as the Preview button). The little down-arrow is gone. (2) Forms — the Custom CSS box on a form is now the single source of truth for that form's styling. Before, each Save added your CSS as a new block stacked on top of the old one, so removing a line didn't actually remove it (a red border you added and then deleted would 'stick'). Now Save replaces the form's style block with exactly what's in the box — add, edit, or delete freely and the live page matches. (Also hardened so this can never touch the rest of the page's markup.) (3) Revisions fixed, and page versions are now backed up in GitHub. First the fix: the View and Restore buttons never actually did anything — the button's click handler was malformed by a quoting bug, so clicking it silently errored out — and editors couldn't open the Revisions panel at all (it was gated to admins). Both are fixed: View now opens the saved version in a new tab and Restore redeploys it to the live page, for admins and editors alike. Second, the backup: every time you Save a page the CMS already stored a full snapshot behind those buttons; those snapshots (and the revision index) are now also mirrored into your site's GitHub repository — so your page version history lives in the repo, your backup of record, and survives even if the server is rebuilt. Older snapshots are pruned from GitHub too, so the repo stays tidy. (This uses the same GitHub repo + token connection that already publishes your pages — set it in Settings; the mirror is best-effort and never blocks a save.) (4) reCAPTCHA is now a proper add-on in the Plugins section (like a Gravity Forms add-on) instead of being buried in Site Foundry: turn it on, paste your two Google keys once, and every form on the site is protected — with an at-a-glance Active/Off badge, a one-click 'Turn off', and a link to grab your keys. Previous (1.14.38) — Editors get Forms + Revisions, and the Revisions history works for everyone. (1) Editor accounts now include Forms (build forms, view submissions) and Revisions (view history, restore versions) by default — no admin toggle needed. An admin can still switch either off for a specific editor. (2) Fixed — the Revisions list was admin-only even when the Revisions module was granted; now anyone with that module sees it. (3) Fixed — the 'View' button on a revision opened a blank tab (the preview window was opened a moment too late, so the browser's pop-up blocker killed it). It now opens reliably; Restore is unchanged. Previous (1.14.37) — Already-launched sites keep sending form email with zero setup. If a site still has its old contact-form handler (a send.php with SMTP credentials) from before it was moved onto the CMS, the CMS now reads those mail credentials straight out of that file — so its contact form keeps emailing without you touching a single config file on that site. (The old send.php is read as text and never run; anything you set in config.secret.php still takes priority.) Previous (1.14.36) — Contact-form email is more forgiving to set up, plus a cleaner config template. When a site sends form email over SMTP, the 'From' address now uses your configured mg_from; if that's left blank it falls back to your SMTP login (always a valid sender) instead of the built-in example address — which the mail service would reject. So a form no longer silently fails just because the From line wasn't filled in. The private-config template (config.secret.example.php) was also rewritten to be much easier to follow, with copy-across instructions for moving a site off an old PHPMailer 'send.php' (which fields map where). Previous (1.14.35) — One-step publishing — 'Save' now applies to every page, no second click. Setting up Analytics used to take two steps: save your tracking IDs (or custom code), then hunt for a separate 'Apply Tracking to All Pages' button. Now clicking Save publishes it to every page in one action — the extra button is gone, and the buttons read 'Save & Apply to All Pages' so it's clear what they do. The Favicon works the same way: uploading (or removing) it now updates every page automatically instead of making you click a second 'Apply to all pages' button. ('Remove from All Pages' for tracking is still there for when you want to strip it.) Previous (1.14.34) — Form builder: a header, visual style controls, and content blocks (Gravity-Forms-style). (1) Header — a form now has its own Heading and Intro text (in the builder's 'Form Header' card) that show above the fields. When you convert a form that's already on a page, the CMS now keeps the heading/intro that sat with it instead of dropping them. (2) Styles — a new 'Styles' card lets you set the form's background color, field corner radius, button color, label color, and input border color, and toggle 'Show field labels' on or off (when off, each field's label becomes its placeholder) — all without writing any CSS. (The Custom CSS box is still there for advanced tweaks, applied on top.) (3) Content blocks — the Add Field palette has a new 'Layout & Content' group with Heading and Text blocks (plus the existing Section Break and HTML Block), so you can add titles and explanatory text between fields. Everything shows up on the live page when you Save, and re-saving pushes the new look to every page the form is on. Previous (1.14.33) — api.php now updates itself, so server-side fixes reach every site automatically. Until now, admin/api.php was the one engine file that never auto-updated — so fixes that live in it (like the contact-form fix, SMTP sending, and clean URLs) had to be uploaded to each site by hand. Because api.php no longer contains any secrets (they're all read from the private config.secret.php file), it can now ride the same automatic updater as the rest of the CMS. IMPORTANT — one last manual step: deploy THIS release's admin/api.php to each site once by hand; from then on api.php updates on its own along with everything else. Safety: the updater will only overwrite api.php on sites that use config.secret.php — if a site still has its secrets written directly inside api.php, the update is refused for that file so nothing is ever wiped (move those secrets into config.secret.php to turn auto-update on for it). Previous (1.14.32) — Fix — side-by-side (half-width) form fields now actually show up on your live pages. Two things were stopping them: (1) pages where a form was placed before the half-width feature kept an older copy of the form's built-in styling, and the CMS wasn't refreshing it — so the new side-by-side layout had no CSS to work with. Now, every time you Save a form, the CMS refreshes that styling on each page the form appears on. (2) Some site themes were overriding the form layout and flattening the fields back into one column; the side-by-side rules are now enforced (scoped and marked important) so a theme can't override them. If your fields are still stacked after this update, open the form and click Save once to push the fix onto your pages. Previous (1.14.31) — Per-page CSS editor now shows the page's existing styling. Open any page's settings (the gear on a page), and the Custom CSS box is pre-filled with the CSS that's already on that page — pulled from the page's own <style> blocks — so you can see and edit your page's styling in one place instead of starting from a blank box. Anything you type is layered on top as an override (so your changes win over the original) and is written into the page file when you Save the page. The box only shows your page's real CSS: the CMS's own managed styles and the shared contact-form styling are left out. (This complements the per-form Custom CSS box from 1.14.30 — that one styles a specific form; this one styles the whole page.) Previous (1.14.30) — Form editor now updates the live form, plus a form CSS editor. (1) When you edit a form and click Save, the CMS re-renders that form everywhere it's already placed on your site — so field changes and styling actually show up on the page (previously an edit only saved inside the CMS and the page kept the old form). (2) The form builder has a new Custom CSS box: style the form yourself (target .foundry-form) to change the button, input borders, spacing, colors, etc. — it's saved with the form and applied wherever the form appears. Previous (1.14.29) — Form builder: drag-and-drop, side-by-side fields, and rounded inputs. Grab the handle (⠿) on any field to drag it up or down and reorder your form. Each field now has a Full width / Half width choice — two half-width fields sit next to each other, so you can build layouts like First Name | Last Name and Phone | Email with a full-width Message and Submit below. Form inputs also have nicely rounded corners now. (Building or re-saving a form applies the new layout; on phones, side-by-side fields stack automatically.) Previous (1.14.28) — Critical fix — contact form submissions are now received. The server was rejecting every public form submission (visitors don't have an admin login), so nothing reached the CMS: no entries, no email notifications. This fixes the form endpoint so submissions come through, get saved, and appear in Forms → Entries. IMPORTANT: this is a server-side fix in api.php — deploy the updated admin/api.php to each site for forms to start working (api.php never auto-updates). Previous (1.14.27) — Form entries fixes + convert existing forms. (1) Submissions now always show up in Entries, even ones that came in without a form ID — they're grouped under 'Uncategorized' so nothing is hidden. (2) New: turn a form that's already on one of your pages into a managed form. In the Forms tab click 'Convert a page form', pick the form the CMS found, review the fields it detected, and Save — the CMS rebuilds it as a proper form and swaps it onto the page, so from then on its submissions are captured and appear in Entries (and export to CSV). This is the piece that was missing when a site was moved onto the CMS: the original contact form kept posting to its old handler, so nothing reached the CMS. IMPORTANT: for captured submissions to be emailed to you, set up SMTP or Mailgun in the site's config (see 1.14.26). Previous (1.14.26) — Contact forms can now send email through SMTP. If a site uses an SMTP mail service (like Mailgun's SMTP), the CMS can send form submissions straight through it — just add the SMTP host, username, and password to the site's private config file. This makes moving an existing site's contact form onto the CMS much easier: the credentials it already had keep working, no separate API key needed. (Setup is server-side; nothing changes in how you use the CMS day to day.) Previous (1.14.25) — Media Library: a much nicer alt-text editor. Clicking an image now opens a full-size popup showing the whole photo, with a large, easy-to-use alt-text box and left/right arrows to move through your library. Saving (or clicking 'Save & next') writes the alt text onto your pages automatically — the separate 'Push alt to pages' button is gone. Only images you actually change are re-applied, so paging through is quick. Previous (1.14.24) — Alt text in the Media Library. Click any image in the Media Library to add alt text — a short description of the image used by screen readers and search engines (good for accessibility and SEO). It's saved with the image, and the new 'Push alt to pages' button writes it onto every matching image across all your pages at once — it finds the image wherever it's used and updates its alt text, while leaving unrelated images alone. Your alt text is kept even when you re-scan the media library. Previous (1.14.23) — Form entries + CSV export. The Forms tab now shows how many submissions each form has received; click that number to open all of a form's entries in a table (newest first), with a 'Download CSV' button to export them to a spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets). Submissions that came in from a form not built in the CMS — like an existing hand-coded contact form on your site — are surfaced too, so no entries get missed. (Entries were already being captured on the server; this adds the viewing + downloading.) Previous (1.14.22) — Fix — the accessibility widget no longer overlaps the reCAPTCHA badge. On pages with reCAPTCHA (which puts a small badge in a bottom corner), the ADAptify accessibility button now automatically moves up to sit just above the badge instead of on top of it. It only shifts when it shares a corner with the badge — otherwise it stays exactly where you placed it — and it adjusts even if the badge loads a moment after the page. Previous (1.14.21) — Team onboarding for agency-managed sites (optional, server-configured). Lets your team self-provision editor logins across client sites: the first time someone signs in with their agency email and the shared onboarding password, an editor account is created for them and they're prompted to set their own password. It never overrides an existing account and only ever grants editor-level access. There's no change to how you use the CMS day to day. Previous (1.14.20) — Fix — clean (no-.html) page links now work automatically. When your site uses short, extensionless web addresses (like /contact instead of /contact.html), the CMS now installs the matching rule on your web server for you, so those links open the right page instead of a 'Not Found' error. It runs automatically when you open the CMS and whenever you use SEO → 'Apply to all pages'. IMPORTANT: this needs the updated admin/api.php deployed to your site once (api.php never updates itself) — until it's deployed, add the clean-URL rewrite block to your site's root .htaccess by hand. This is what caused extensionless links to 404 on a live site: the CMS had rewritten every link to the short form, but the server had never been told to serve /page from /page.html. Previous (1.14.19) — Maintenance — automatic updates now come from the agency's renamed shared account. The account that publishes CMS engine updates was renamed to YourDigitalGroup, so every deployed site's auto-updater has been re-pointed to it. Nothing changes in how you use the CMS; this just makes sure future updates keep arriving. (The 'Repository Override' and 'Create New Repo' placeholders were updated to the new account name too.) Previous (1.14.18) — Style individual words (inline text formatting). Double-click any text on your page to edit it, then select a word or phrase and a small toolbar appears right above it — set bold, italic, or underline, change the font or size, or pick a color for just that selection (like Google Docs). Press Escape or click elsewhere to finish. Chosen fonts load automatically so they look right in the editor and on the live page. Previous (1.14.17) — Icon library + SVG uploads. In the page editor, select an element and click 'Insert an icon' to open a searchable library of common icons (contact, arrows, checkmarks, shopping, social, and more) — pick one and it drops into your page, ready to recolor and resize like any other element. The page automatically loads the icon font so your icons show up both in the editor and on the live site. You can also upload your own SVG graphics through the Media Library (and the editor's image picker) and use them like any other image. Previous (1.14.16) — Dark mode fix — the editor's Markup button is readable again. In dark mode it was showing white text on a light button; its text now stays dark on the light button (and white on the orange hover), so it reads clearly in both light and dark. Previous (1.14.15) — Site-wide heading styles, friendlier wording, and editor polish. (1) The Design tab now has Heading Styles — choose the font, size, weight, alignment, and color for your titles (H1 through H6) across your whole site, then click Apply to all pages. A single page can still set its own heading look in that page's settings. (2) The help text throughout the CMS has been rewritten in plain, everyday language — less technical jargon, more about what each setting actually does for you. (3) In the page editor's Background control, 'No fill' is now a swatch (a box with a red diagonal line) that sits right in the color row, like a design app, instead of a separate button. (4) Removed the dashboard's 'Team activity' and 'Top performer' placeholder panels so your pages list uses the full width. Previous (1.14.14) — Clean URLs are now always on. The brief on/off toggle from 1.14.13 is gone — the CMS simply always uses extensionless URLs, so there's nothing to switch. After deploying your server's URL rewrite, run 'Apply to all pages' in SEO to rewrite existing pages. Previous (1.14.13) — Clean URLs — drop the .html for better SEO. Page URLs are now extensionless (e.g. /about instead of /about.html): each page gets one canonical, search-friendly address, the sitemap and the page's own internal links use that clean form, and any old .html link redirects to it automatically. Run 'Apply to all pages' in SEO to rewrite your existing pages' links and canonicals to the clean form. This needs the matching URL rewrite on your server, included with this release's site configuration. Your files stay plain .html on disk — only the public URL changes. Previous (1.14.12) — Editor controls: collapsible panels, border, background options, and a sticky-header toggle. (1) The element properties panel is now organized into collapsible sections — Text Style, Spacing & Style, Border, Size, Anchor, Hover, and more — so you can fold away what you're not using. Each section remembers whether you left it open or closed. (2) New Border controls: width, style (solid / dashed / dotted / double), and color; setting a width auto-applies a solid style so the border shows right away. Like the Size controls, borders honor the per-device cascade (set them differently for desktop, tablet, or mobile). (3) The Background control gains two options: a 'No fill' button to make a section transparent, and an 'Image' button to set a background image from the Media Library (with a 'Remove image' once one is set). (4) Header elements get a 'Sticky' toggle — turn it on so the header stays visible as visitors scroll, then use Save to All to apply it across every page. Previous (1.14.11) — Dark mode for the CMS. The admin now has a dark theme — open the profile menu (the avatar in the top-right) and flip the Dark mode switch. Your choice is remembered on this device and applied before the screen draws, so there's no flash of the light theme when you reload; if you've never picked one, it follows your operating system's light/dark setting. The whole interface re-themes — sidebar, top bar, panels, cards, inputs, modals, dropdowns, and the editor canvas — with the ember accent kept throughout. The page preview itself stays on light 'paper' so the website you're editing always looks the way visitors will see it. Previous (1.14.10) — Responsive editing fixes + size controls + jump-to-code. (1) Style changes now apply correctly across devices. Setting a size, spacing, or other style on Desktop or Tablet no longer locks that value so it can't be overridden on smaller screens. Every device value is now written into one managed stylesheet in cascade order Desktop -> Tablet -> Mobile (same specificity), so a value set on Mobile wins on phones; a value set on Tablet applies to tablet and phones unless Mobile overrides it; and a Desktop value applies everywhere unless Tablet or Mobile changes it. Any older inline value that was trapping a property at one breakpoint is migrated into the Desktop rule the first time you edit that property, so margins, padding, width and height finally respond on mobile. (2) New Size controls in the element panel: Width, Max width, Height and Max height inputs for any element - divs, images, videos, columns - accepting px, %, auto, and the like. They honor the same per-device cascade, so you can size something one way on desktop and another on mobile, and 'Reset to natural size' now clears all four across every device. (3) Switching a selected element to Code view now jumps to its markup: open the Code editor with an element selected and it scrolls straight to where that element is in the source and briefly highlights it, so you don't have to hunt for it. Previous (1.14.9) — Pages can no longer silently vanish from the editor list. The list is built from pages.json plus a server scan of your files; if that scan fails or times out (for example on a site whose data/ folder has filled up with revision snapshots) while pages.json is sparse, the list could rebuild empty and look like every page was dropped. The CMS now keeps a 'last known good' snapshot of your page list and, whenever a read it depends on fails, restores any page that was dropped (still respecting pages you've hidden, and still clearing on a genuine empty result). The snapshot is captured on every import, page save, and healthy load, so it survives refreshes — and intentional deletions are kept in sync so they don't come back. (The underlying cause on affected sites is the data/ revision build-up; the v1.14.7 api.php change stops the scanner reading data/ — deploy it to clear the root cause.) Previous (1.14.8) — Imports no longer use the Markup engine — no API tokens are spent when importing. Bringing pages into the CMS (scanning from the server or dropping a ZIP) is now a plain, fast operation: each page is read and stored as-is, with its CSS still captured so styling is preserved, and no per-page AI calls. The 'Optimize markup for editing' option and the automatic AI section-splitting have both been removed from the import flow (do that refactoring in Claude Design instead). The in-editor Markup tools are unchanged. Previous (1.14.7) — Imported pages no longer drop off — root-cause fix. The CMS writes a revision snapshot on every page save, stored in your site's data/ folder (data/rev_rev_<timestamp>.html). The page scanner was reading that folder, so those revision files showed up as 'pages' and then vanished on the next load — the editor correctly refuses to keep data files — which looked exactly like your imported pages being dropped. Now: the page scanner no longer looks inside data/ at all (it never did belong there), the page list already ignores anything under data/, and — as a safety net — if the page list ever fails to load because of a momentary network or session hiccup, the CMS keeps the pages already on screen instead of blanking the list. NOTE: the scanner change is in api.php, which is a manual deploy — deploy the updated admin/api.php to get the full root-cause fix; until then the page list still hides those files client-side, so you won't see them, and a hard-refresh ensures you're on this build. Previous (1.14.6) — Import fixes + click-anywhere rows. (1) Site Foundry no longer offers — or keeps — files that aren't real, editable pages. Data files, CMS revision snapshots (e.g. data/rev_rev_1782506528689.html), and backup files are filtered out of the scan and skipped on import (the scan now shows how many system/backup files were skipped). This is what was causing imported pages to 'disappear' after you left the editor: those entries were revision files the editor can't open, so they dropped off on the next scan — the pages list is now stable, and any such junk already saved in your pages list is cleaned up automatically on load. (2) Click anywhere on a row to act on it: the Site Foundry import scan rows now toggle their checkbox on a row click (not just the box), and the Users, Posts, and Forms lists open the editor on a row click (not just the Edit button) — controls inside a row (Edit, copy-shortcode, entry links) still do their own thing. Previous (1.14.5) — Import progress loader. Site Foundry's 'Import from Server' now shows a clear, animated progress indicator while it runs — a spinner, 'Importing page X of N' with the live step (reading / optimizing / extracting sections / saving), and a progress bar that fills as pages complete — plus a spinner on the Import button. So it's obvious the import is working and not stuck, and the loader clears the moment it finishes. Previous (1.14.4) — Import reliability fix. Site Foundry imports no longer get stuck on the 'Importing…' spinner. The per-page Markup calls (block extraction, and the optional page optimization) now have a client-side timeout, so a slow or unavailable Markup engine can never freeze an import — that page falls back to importing as-is and the run continues. The 'Optimize markup for editing' option is now OFF by default (opt-in) on both Site Foundry tabs: running the full optimization on every page made imports slow and look hung, so it's now a deliberate choice — turn it on when you want it (it processes each page through Markup, so it takes longer). Previous (1.14.3) — Editor image previews + cleaner color swatches. The 'Site Colors' captions under the editor's color pickers are gone — just the clickable swatches remain, sitting directly under each color field. And image previews in the page editor are now clear, square cards: when an image is selected, its preview at the top of the properties panel is a square box (was a short strip that squished wide logos/banners to ~50px tall), and the image picker's tiles use a square thumbnail with object-fit: contain so the full image is always visible regardless of its shape — much easier to see and pick. Previous (1.14.2) — Site color palette in the editor. Wherever you set a color in the page editor — text color, background, and button/link hover colors — a 'site colors' palette now appears beneath the picker: the colors actually used on the page, listed most-used to least-used, with your brand colors always included. Click a swatch to apply it instantly, so it's easy to stay on-palette without copying hex codes. (The palette reads the live page's rendered colors and refreshes when you open a different page.) Previous (1.14.1) — Editor freshness + SEO accuracy + Design polish. (1) The page editor now always opens the freshest published version of a page — it cache-busts the file when loading, so a stale browser-cached copy can no longer show up after you publish and then refresh the CMS. (2) The SEO analysis is fairer and more technical, closer to a Lighthouse audit. Keyword matching is no longer punctuation-literal: a focus keyword like 'doors vermillion sd' now correctly matches a title of 'Doors, Vermillion, SD' (and partial word matches count, so it isn't overly strict about exact phrasing). The checklist adds technical health checks read from the live page — descriptive (non-placeholder) title, indexable (not noindex), canonical available, exactly one H1, all images have alt text, and enough content — alongside the keyword checks. Generated SEO now produces a clean primary keyword (lowercase, no punctuation) whose words appear naturally in the title and description. (3) The Design tab's Primary and Secondary brand-color pickers are now proper squares instead of wide, short boxes. (4) Client-facing wording no longer mentions 'Claude' or an API key: Site Foundry shows a plain 'Markup isn't enabled yet' note instead of 'No Claude API key — add it in Settings', and the editor's status and error messages use 'Markup'. Previous (1.14.0) — Automatic markup optimization in Site Foundry. When you bring a site into the CMS — either by scanning the files on your server or by dropping a ZIP — each page's markup can now be optimized for the editor: semantic landmarks (header / nav / main / footer), named sections with clear ids, the standard FAQ/accordion structure, proper headings (one H1 per page), real image tags with alt text, a single place for CSS, brand-color variables, and clean file naming (index.html for the homepage and a single .html extension for the rest). A new 'Optimize markup for editing' toggle (on by default, shown when Markup is available) controls it on both Site Foundry tabs. Your design is never changed: every optimized page is run through a fidelity check first — if its visible text or its stylesheet didn't survive intact, the original page is kept untouched — and the page's scripts are preserved verbatim across the round trip. When scanning from the server, the optimized page is written back to the server file (and pushed to GitHub when connected) so the editor opens the improved version; pages too large to optimize safely, or that don't pass the check, are imported as-is. Previous (1.13.8) — Faithful mobile/tablet preview + reachable controls. The Tablet and Mobile previews now emulate a real device like the browser's inspector: the page renders at the device's true CSS width (mobile 390×844, tablet 768×1024) so its own responsive CSS reflows exactly as on the device, and the whole frame scales down to fit the editor — much closer to what you see on your phone. Editing handles now stay grabbable even when an element runs off the edge of a narrow preview: the resize handles clamp to the visible edge instead of disappearing off-screen (and the move/duplicate/delete + size controls in the right panel are always available). Previous (1.13.7) — Tidy-up: the Hover controls in the editor are now labeled just 'Background' and 'Text' (they already sit under the 'Hover' heading, so the repeated word is gone). Previous (1.13.6) — Editor breathing room + privacy-page layout. Generated pages (privacy policy, new pages) now place the content in a centered column with real side margins whose layout is locked with !important so the site's own CSS can't stretch it edge-to-edge, and the real header and footer are kept inside their original wrapper (so their styling holds) — the content between them is swapped rather than rebuilt. The main left sidebar now auto-collapses when you enter the page editor (more room for the preview) and expands again elsewhere; you can still toggle it any time with the button by the logo. The desktop preview is now resizable — grab the orange handle at its bottom-right corner and drag to set any size (switching device resets it). Previous (1.13.5) — Font picker upgrades for per-page heading styles. Each font in the dropdown now renders in its own typeface (the curated fonts load into the admin so you can see them). Added a 'More fonts…' option that opens a searchable picker for the entire Google Fonts library — type to filter, each result previews in its font, and you can apply any font by exact name even if it's not in the list (the browse list is fetched live, with the built-in set as a fallback). Picked fonts are added to the dropdown and load on the page automatically. Previous (1.13.4) — Editor polish + fixes. The page editor's properties panel no longer has an 'Apply Changes' button — every edit (text, color, size, weight, alignment, spacing, links, anchors, image source/alt) now applies automatically as you make it. Text alignment now applies reliably (written with !important so a theme's own rules can't override it). Buttons and links gain Hover controls — set a hover background and text color, saved as a per-element :hover rule in the page. Per-page heading styles now cover H1–H6 (was H1–H3) and add a Google Fonts picker; the chosen fonts are loaded on the page automatically. New pages and the privacy-policy page now sit in a centered content section with real side margins and room below the header (no more overlap), while still pulling in the site's real header and footer. Fixed the Restore button in Site Foundry → Manage Pages (it wasn't firing due to a quote-escaping bug) and removed the now-redundant remove buttons there. Reworded client-facing messages so they no longer reference internal system files. Previous (1.13.3) — Subtle version stamp in the bottom-left of the sidebar (small gray monospace, e.g. 'v1.13.3') so you can see which build is loaded at a glance — it reads the live CMS_VERSION and hides when the sidebar is collapsed. Previous (1.13.2) — New + generated pages now inherit the site header/footer. The privacy-policy generator and the New Page action used to emit a standalone document with no header/nav/footer; they now clone a real page (its head/CSS + header + nav + footer) and swap in the new content, so created pages match the rest of the site (falls back to a standalone doc only if there's no donor page). Resize anchoring fixed properly: a normal drag now keeps the OPPOSITE edge/corner fixed (drag the bottom-right and the left/top stay put) no matter how the element is centered — it probes how margin maps to on-screen position, then measures and corrects so the anchor holds even for flex- or text-align-centered elements (the old margin-pin didn't work on those, so it looked symmetric). Hold Cmd (Mac) / Ctrl (PC) for symmetric resize from the center. Site Foundry → Manage Pages: removed the redundant 'Remove from CMS' buttons (removal lives in the editor's gear → Page Settings); the page list + Restore for hidden pages remain. The sidebar 'Pages' count (and dashboard stat) now update immediately whenever a page is removed, deleted, restored, or added. Design tab: brand colors are now Primary + Secondary, bound to the --brand-color-primary / --brand-color-secondary CSS variables. They auto-fill from the site's CSS (and empty Contact/Social fields auto-fill from the site's links); 'Apply colors to all pages' injects a managed :root override into every page so the variables update site-wide. Previous (1.13.1) — Fixes. Values funnel down the cascade — Desktop applies everywhere, Tablet overrides tablet+mobile (≤1024px), Mobile overrides mobile (≤600px) — so you only override what differs. The properties panel shows a banner telling you which device you're editing. Flex containers get a Direction control (row / row-reverse / column / column-reverse); set it per device to reverse or stack columns on phones. Overrides are saved into a managed <style id=fourge-responsive> block in the page and the element classes, so they reconstruct correctly when you reopen the page (and a new override never wipes existing ones).
